Letta opens with the constraint that drives everything: "LLMs are sensitive to what's in their context window. They can't reason about information they don't 'see' at inference time." A vector database full of past conversation history is useless if the retrieval doesn't surface the right snippet into the actual prompt. The job of a memory system isn't to store things — it's to **compile context windows** from stored state on demand.

## The structure of a memory block

Each block has four components:

1. **Label** — identifies purpose: `human`, `persona`, `knowledge`, `current_task`, etc. The label is also the key the agent uses to reference the block.
2. **Value** — the actual string content. Complex structures (lists, dicts) are serialized as strings so the model can read them directly.
3. **Size limit** — character or token cap. This is load-bearing: it forces the agent to summarize/curate rather than accumulate forever.
4. **Description** — optional metadata describing how the block should be used.

Blocks can be **read-only** (developer-controlled, like a system persona) or **agent-editable** (the agent modifies them via tool calls during its loop). The split matters because some context — the persona, the security constraints — shouldn't drift, while other context — the user's current task, recent decisions — should evolve.

## The compilation step

The critical mechanism Letta highlights is **context compilation**. At inference time, Letta assembles the prompt by reading current block values from its persistence layer and templating them (with Jinja) into a structured system prompt. So the agent's working memory at step T isn't whatever happened to be in the context window at step T-1; it's a freshly compiled view of the persisted block values. This decouples "what the agent remembers" from "what's currently visible to the model."

This is also what enables **multi-agent sharing**: multiple agents can read the same block by ID, so a shared `decisions.md`-style fact is genuinely shared rather than copied. And every block has a unique `block_id`, so external systems can update agent memory directly via API — useful for piping in real-world events without going through a conversation turn.

## Why this is the right primitive

Most early agent memory systems were either too coarse (one giant scratchpad) or too fine (vector chunks with retrieval). Memory blocks sit in the middle: **labeled, sized, semantically named units that the agent and developer both understand**. The naming alone is a meaningful improvement — when an agent has tools like `update_block(label="current_task", value="...")`, its self-management becomes explicit and auditable.

The size limits enforce a discipline that vector DBs alone never do: you can't grow forever, so you have to decide what's worth keeping. This mirrors how human working memory works — limited, structured, requiring active curation.

## Practical takeaway

If you're designing an agent memory system, **don't equate "store everything" with "remember everything"**. Adopt the memory-block discipline: name the slots your agent needs (persona, user model, current task, recent decisions, scratchpad), bound each one, decide which are agent-editable, and build a compilation step that templates them into the prompt. Vector retrieval can sit *behind* memory blocks (the block content is summarized retrieval), but it should not be the entire memory layer. The deeper insight from Letta is that **memory is a context-compilation problem, not a storage problem** — and once you frame it that way, the design space gets dramatically clearer.

Simon Willison's high-conviction take that **Claude Skills may end up more important than MCP**. The argument is unfashionably simple: Skills work by leveraging exactly what LLMs already do well — read text and execute code — while MCP defines a sophisticated protocol that's expensive to integrate. Skills are smaller, more portable, and not model-specific, which Willison thinks will lead to a "Cambrian explosion" of agent extensions.

A Claude Skill is, mechanically, three things bundled together:

1. **A Markdown document** with YAML frontmatter containing metadata (name, description, when to use it).
2. **Optional executable scripts** in any language the runtime supports.
3. **Instructions** the model reads and follows when the skill is triggered.

That's it. No protocol, no server, no schema negotiation. The skill is a folder of files. The model loads its frontmatter and short description into context permanently; if the user's request matches, the model reads the full Markdown body and runs the scripts.

## Why simplicity is the feature, not a limitation

Willison's central observation: "each skill only takes up a few dozen extra tokens, with the full details only loaded in should the user request a task that the skill can help solve." The economics are excellent. You can install hundreds of skills and pay almost nothing in tokens until one of them actually fires. Compare to MCP, where tool descriptions sit in context whether or not they're used, eating tokens on every turn.

The deeper point: "The core simplicity of the skills design is why I'm so excited about it." The skill format is essentially **a folder you commit to a repo** — version-controllable, diffable, shareable as a tarball, no runtime infrastructure required. The activation mechanism is just text matching and code execution, which are exactly the things every coding model is already extremely good at.

## The model-independence argument

The killer Willison observation about Skills' future: unlike MCP, Skills aren't model-dependent. They "function with 'other models' like Gemini CLI or Codex CLI." Because Skills are just Markdown + scripts, any agent harness that knows how to read files and execute commands can use them. There's no MCP-equivalent protocol negotiation, no client/server lifecycle. You can take a Claude Skill and use it with a Codex agent unchanged — or vice versa.

This is exactly the property that lets a tooling category grow: low coupling to any one runtime. Willison's prediction is that the ease of authoring Skills will drive **a Cambrian explosion in Skills which will make this year's MCP rush look pedestrian by comparison**. Whether or not that's right, the direction of the bet is well-grounded.

## How this changes the agent-extension picture

MCP and Skills aren't strictly competitors — they operate at different layers. MCP is good for **live, structured tool access to running services** (your database, your APIs, your filesystem). Skills are good for **packaged know-how plus scripts** that can be checked in next to your code. The choice is roughly: "do I want the agent to have a typed, validated channel into a live system" (MCP) versus "do I want to give the agent a Markdown-shaped recipe with executable bits" (Skill). For many use cases, the Skill form is dramatically simpler and good enough.

## Practical takeaway

If you've been waiting to expose internal knowledge to your agents and bouncing off MCP's complexity, **try a Skill first**. The barrier to entry is a folder, a YAML header, and a Markdown body — possibly plus a shell script. The portability across runtimes is real, and the token economics are vastly better than tool-stuffing the system prompt. The broader lesson Willison is articulating is that **agent extension wins go to the format that respects what LLMs already do well**, and reading text plus executing code is the most native combination available. Bet on simple primitives.

Hugging Face's full-recipe writeup for turning a small VLM into a respectable **GUI computer-use agent** via post-training. The headline result: SmolVLM2-2.2B-Instruct goes from **0.47%** to **61.71%** on ScreenSpot-v2 — a roughly 130x improvement on a benchmark for clicking and interacting with UI elements — using only supervised fine-tuning, with everything open-sourced. The scale of the jump is the point. This isn't a 5% incremental gain; it's a complete capability emergence from a near-zero baseline. The recipe also fits in roughly one notebook, runs on accessible hardware, and uses open datasets. Anyone serious about computer-use agents now has a credible path to a useful small model without lab-scale resources. ## The two-phase recipe **Phase 1 — Zero to Perception (0.47% → 41.27%).** Trained on `smolagents/aguvis-stage-1` (400K samples), 2 epochs, image resolution 1152px, **normalized coordinates** (0–1 range). The format is brutally simple: `{"user": "click on more button", "assistant": "click(x=0.8875, y=0.2281)"}`. The model learns to ground a textual instruction in a screen coordinate. The team notes the baseline was "a clean slate" — the model had zero existing UI grounding. **Phase 2 — Perception to Cognition (41.27% → 61.71%).** Continued from the Phase 1 checkpoint on `smolagents/aguvis-stage-2` (784K samples), 2 epochs, same resolution. The new ingredient is **explicit `` blocks** wrapping the model's reasoning before the action: `Click on the link labeled 'Judith Lauand...'click(x=0.41, y=0.178)`. Reasoning-aware data adds another 20+ points on top of perception alone. ## The action-space converter One of the most reusable contributions is the **unified action space**. Different datasets use different function signatures — `mobile.swipe`, `pyautogui.click`, `mobile.long_press` — and the team built a converter that normalizes everything to one vocabulary (`click`, `type`, `press`, `swipe`). The components: a function parser, an action-conversion preprocessor, and a flexible `ActionSpaceConverter` you can configure with `ActionMapping` and `ParameterMapping` objects. This is the kind of infrastructure that gets overlooked in papers but is what actually makes the training pipeline work, and the team open-sources it. ## The normalized-coordinates lesson A notable ablation: at 1152px resolution, **pixel coordinates score 4.32% while normalized coordinates score 33.72%** — a roughly 8x gap on perception alone. The reasoning is mechanical: "Using raw pixel coordinates ties them to a single image size. VLMs often resize images, causing pixel coordinates to break." Normalized coordinates are robust across resizes. This is a small finding that should be considered settled engineering practice for anyone training UI-grounded models. ## What's released Everything: the training notebook (`recipe.ipynb`), the final model (`smolagents/SmolVLM2-2.2B-Instruct-Agentic-GUI`), both stage datasets, the action-space converter library, and a demo space. This is the rare case where a blog post is functionally equivalent to a paper plus all artifacts. ## Practical takeaway If you want to build a GUI computer-use agent in 2026 and you can't run a frontier model on-device, **this is the path**. Start with a small open VLM, use the two-phase SFT recipe (perception then reasoning), normalize your coordinates, and standardize your action space. You won't beat GPT-5 or Claude Opus, but you'll get a usable 60%+ ScreenSpot-v2 model that runs cheaply and locally. The deeper lesson — applicable beyond GUI work — is that **carefully curated, reasoning-annotated data with a unified action vocabulary can lift small models dramatically with vanilla SFT, no RL required**. The 2.2B parameter count matters: the recipe scales down to hardware most teams have, which is exactly what's needed for the next wave of practical computer-use products.

OpenAI's official prompting guide for **gpt-5.3-codex**, the coding-specialized variant of GPT-5. The post is dense, more a reference manual than a think-piece, and worth reading carefully because it documents the exact mechanisms the model was trained to expect — `apply_patch`, parallel tool calls, compaction, preamble messages, CFGs, personality modes. If you're building a Codex-based agent, this is the load-bearing document.

The model itself is positioned as a step up in **efficiency and autonomy**: it "uses fewer thinking tokens to accomplish a task," with "medium" reasoning effort as the recommended default for interactive coding work. It's tuned for multi-hour, continuous task execution without user babysitting, and first-class compaction support is what makes that practical.

## apply_patch

The model has been heavily post-trained on a specific diff format. You should use the built-in `apply_patch` tool in the Responses API rather than rolling your own. The alternative — letting the model emit free-form file rewrites — is wasteful and error-prone. For custom needs, the guide describes a CFG-based approach using Lark grammar definitions to constrain the model's diff output. The takeaway: **respect the diff format the model was trained on**.

## Parallel tool calling

The model supports `multi_tool_use.parallel` for batching tool calls. The prompting guidance is to plan all needed resources before calling and batch operations "even across different file locations." Sequential file reads should be avoided unless logically necessary. The result is dramatically lower latency on multi-file tasks because reads can be fanned out.

## Compaction

The `/responses/compact` endpoint creates compressed context representations, enabling multi-turn conversations and long-running agent trajectories that exceed normal context limits. Encrypted content items are passed to subsequent requests so the model can resume work without re-reading everything. This is the mechanism that makes multi-hour Codex sessions work in practice — without compaction, you'd hit the wall.

## Phase metadata and preamble messages

The Responses API includes a `phase` field on assistant items: `commentary` for progress updates, `final_answer` for closing summaries. The guide is specific that **preserving phase metadata is critical** — strip it and performance degrades. This is a non-obvious operational gotcha: when you serialize/replay conversation history, don't drop the phase field.

Preamble messages — the short "commentary" outputs the model emits before tool calls — let users see the model's plan in real time. They're cheap and significantly improve UX.

## Personality modes

Two built-in styles: **Friendly** (collaborative, empathetic, warmer language — good for pair-programming) and **Pragmatic** (direct, terse, throughput-optimized — good for automated harness use). Pick one explicitly via system prompt; don't let the model drift between them.

## CFGs and metaprompting

For highly structured outputs (DSLs, custom config formats), you can attach a Lark CFG to a custom tool to constrain the model's output to grammatically valid productions. This is more reliable than after-the-fact validation when the output format is constrained enough to specify.

The guide also recommends **metaprompting for optimization**: ask the model to identify bottlenecks in its own system prompt, particularly delayed first action, verbose status updates, and repetitive phrasing. This is a small but reliable trick — Codex is good at spotting where its own instructions are noisy.

## Best practices the guide is firm on

- Use dedicated tools (`git`, `read_file`, `apply_patch`) before falling back to shell commands.
- Batch parallel reads; avoid sequential file operations.
- Propagate errors explicitly; avoid silent catches.
- Use `apply_patch` for single-file changes; prefer scripting for auto-generated content.
- Maintain strict typing; reuse existing helpers rather than type-asserting.

## Practical takeaway

If you're integrating gpt-5.3-codex, **don't treat it as a generic chat model with tools**. Use the specific mechanisms the OpenAI training pipeline expects: `apply_patch` for diffs, `multi_tool_use.parallel` for fan-out, `/responses/compact` for long sessions, preserve `phase` metadata in your harness. The deeper point is that **modern coding models are starting to expect specific runtime affordances** — they were trained inside particular harnesses, and reproducing those harnesses on the client side is the difference between getting frontier-level performance and getting a worse-than-prior-gen experience.

Browserbase's writeup on rebuilding **Stagehand from a client-side library into a server-side API**, and why that architectural shift unlocks something more important than performance — it unlocks observability. The thesis: when high-level browser automation commands are translated to Chrome DevTools Protocol calls on the client, the *intent* is lost the moment translation happens, and you can't debug what you can't see.

The old architecture worked like this. A developer writes `page.act("click the quickstart button")`. Stagehand, running in the user's process, asks an LLM to translate that into a sequence of CDP commands — `Page.navigate`, `Runtime.evaluate`, `Input.dispatchMouseEvent`. Those low-level messages get sent to the browser. The browser sees the CDP frames; it never sees "click the quickstart button." Session replay shows you the WebSocket traffic, which is technically accurate and entirely useless for diagnosing why the click went wrong. As Browserbase puts it, "The 'why' behind the action is completely lost during the translation process."

## The server-side API design

The new architecture inverts this. High-level commands go straight to Browserbase's managed API, where the translation happens on infrastructure Browserbase controls. The benefits stack:

1. **Intent preserved end-to-end.** The original business goal ("click the quickstart button") is the system's source of truth, not an artifact lost in client-side translation.
2. **Observable LLM traces.** "We are now able to get crucial information on what the user is specifically asking for." Teams see meaningful prompts and responses rather than CDP byte streams.
3. **Actionable debugging.** "You can see exactly what instruction was sent to the LLM, what was returned, and how many cache hits you got." Failure diagnosis goes from "why did the WebSocket send this byte sequence" to "the LLM misinterpreted 'quickstart button' as a different visible button."

Moving translation server-side also enables **caching of repeated workflows** (the same `act` call on the same page can hit a cache), **intelligent self-healing through retries** (failures can be observed and re-prompted server-side), and **automatic model routing** (cheap models for simple commands, expensive models for ambiguous ones).

## Why this matters beyond Stagehand

The pattern generalizes to any system where **a high-level intent gets translated through an LLM into low-level actions**. The temptation is always to do the translation as close to the action surface as possible (in the user's process, near the browser, near the database). The cost is that observability and reliability tooling lives at the wrong layer — you see the post-translation noise, not the pre-translation signal.

The Stagehand team's reframing is essentially that **the LLM call is the primary event to observe**, and everything downstream (CDP frames, DB queries, API calls) is implementation detail. Logging the implementation detail without the originating intent is a known anti-pattern in distributed systems; Browserbase is calling it out for the agent era.

The choice does impose tradeoffs — your LLM call now lives behind a vendor API, which means latency and pricing become Browserbase's problem to manage. But for the bulk of users who weren't going to instrument their own LLM-trace pipeline, the tradeoff is correct: get the observability for free and stop paying the debugging tax.

## Practical takeaway

If you're building any automation layer that uses an LLM to translate from intent to action, **think hard about where the translation should happen**. Client-side translation feels simpler but produces unobservable systems. Server-side translation requires more infrastructure but lets you log, cache, retry, and route intelligently. The lesson is broader than browser automation: anywhere you have a layer that goes from natural language to a structured low-level protocol, the layer should be the most heavily observed component in the stack, because that's where the failures will live. Stagehand's pivot is a clean case study in choosing architecture for observability.

Simon Willison's walkthrough of **Claude Code for web** — Anthropic's containerized async coding agent, the analog to OpenAI's Codex Cloud and Google's Jules. The post is part product tour, part security analysis, and the security commentary is what makes it worth reading. Willison's framing of network access tiers as the primary defense against the "lethal trifecta" is now a standard way to think about agent safety.

Mechanically: you point Claude Code for web at a GitHub repo (via claude.ai/code), pick an environment tier, write a prompt, and the agent runs asynchronously. You can queue additional instructions while it's working. When it finishes, it opens a branch with its changes and optionally files a PR. It's the same Claude Code CLI tool wrapped in a web/mobile interface with a managed container.

## What Willison actually built with it

The practical examples ground the post. He ran three real tasks:

- A **simple HTML tool** (a query-string-stripper utility) deployed via GitHub Pages.
- A **complex benchmark project** comparing MiniJinja against Jinja2 under Python 3.14 free-threading — shell scripts, implementation code, Markdown writeups, matplotlib charts.
- **Documentation refreshes** on outdated project READMEs.

The range matters. This isn't a toy; it can produce a real comparison study including charts, end-to-end.

## Network access tiers as the security primitive

The most important architectural choice is the three-tier network access model:

1. **No network access** — fully isolated. Use for sensitive code where you don't want the agent reaching outbound at all.
2. **Trusted network access** — limited to a curated allowlist of dependency-installation domains (npm, PyPI, GitHub for cloning, etc.). The default for most uses.
3. **Custom environments** — user-defined allowlists, including `*` (anything) for non-sensitive work.

This is the right shape because **network access is the data-exfiltration channel** in the agent threat model. Willison ties this explicitly to the "lethal trifecta" framing (code execution + sensitive data + exfiltration channel — remove any one and the attack fails). "Network isolation is how you remove the data exfiltration leg." Filesystem isolation handles the data-access leg. Code execution can't be removed — that's what the agent is for. So the only safe configuration is to choke the network down hard.

Underneath, the implementation uses the same primitives as local Claude Code sandboxing: **seatbelt on macOS, Bubblewrap on Linux**, plus a proxy server managing outbound domain restrictions. The architectural continuity matters — Anthropic isn't reinventing the security model for the web version; they're applying the same model to a managed container.

## The productivity vs. safety tradeoff, named

Willison's most quotable observation is that **permission-skipping agents in "YOLO mode" (`--dangerously-skip-permissions`) are vastly more productive than those that pause for approvals** — but only sandboxing makes that mode safe. Without the network/filesystem boundaries, YOLO mode is genuinely dangerous; with them, it's the right default. This collapses the apparent tradeoff: you don't have to choose between fast and safe, you have to invest in the boundaries that let you have both.

## Practical takeaway

If you're considering Claude Code for web or any async coding-agent service, **the network tier is the security knob that matters most**. Default to tier 2 (trusted dependency domains only) and only escalate to tier 3 when the task genuinely requires it (e.g., scraping public web data). Combine with branch-based PR review so a human sees diffs before merge. The deeper lesson is that **agent autonomy is a function of the trust boundary you can credibly enforce** — not of how clever the model is. If you can sandbox the agent's network and filesystem properly, you can let it run YOLO and get the productivity wins. If you can't, you're stuck in permission-prompt purgatory. Anthropic has invested in the sandboxing because that's the only way to credibly ship async coding agents to enterprises.

Lance Martin's reflective case study applying **Rich Sutton's "Bitter Lesson"** to his own agent architecture work. The post is unusually honest: Martin built a rigid orchestrator-worker workflow that worked well at the time, then watched it become obsolete as model tool-calling improved, and writes about what he learned from being on the wrong side of the lesson. It's required reading for anyone building agent infrastructure that bets against model improvement.

Sutton's original Bitter Lesson, restated: "General methods that leverage computation are ultimately the most effective, and by a large margin." The lesson is bitter because researchers keep encoding human structure (game-specific features, hand-tuned heuristics) into systems that compute will eventually do better without — chess, vision, speech, and now AI engineering itself.

## The case study

**Late 2024**: Martin builds a web-research system as an **orchestrator-worker workflow**. The orchestrator decomposes a user request into report sections; workers research and write sections in parallel. Critically, he **avoids tool-calling** for reliability — at that point, tool-calling was unreliable enough that hand-structured decomposition was a better bet. The design embeds two assumptions: (a) the orchestrator knows how to decompose research, and (b) the model can't handle dynamic tool calls.

**Late 2024 / early 2025**: Tool-calling improves dramatically. MCP launches and gains momentum. Models start handling research-style tool loops reliably. Martin's rigid architecture can't take advantage — the structural choice that made the system reliable a few months earlier now caps its ceiling.

**Iteration**: Martin moves to a multi-agent system but **initially preserves parallel section-writing**, producing disjointed reports where the sections don't cohere. The diagnosis is that he hadn't actually removed the structural assumption; he'd just renamed it. The fix is moving writing to a **final synthesis step**, allowing flexible upstream research and single-pass coherent report generation.

**Result**: The updated system scores **43.5 on Deep Research Bench** (top 10), achieved without reinforcement learning or massive resources — just by removing structure that the underlying model had outgrown.

## The three lessons for agent engineers

Martin distills three practices:

1. **Understand the assumptions about model capabilities baked into your architecture.** Every architectural choice ("the model can't do X reliably, so I'll structure around it") encodes a snapshot of what the model could do at design time.
2. **Regularly reassess as models improve.** Capability gains are fast; structural assumptions that were correct in Q1 may be obsolete by Q3.
3. **Design for easy reconfiguration.** Use low-level composable primitives rather than rigid frameworks, so swapping in a more-capable model is a refactor, not a rewrite.

Martin also issues a quieter warning about **agent frameworks**: they can inadvertently lock in structure (predefined orchestrator patterns, fixed tool wrappers) that becomes hard to remove later. He advocates composable building blocks over opinionated frameworks — which echoes the same point Anthropic makes in their "Building Effective Agents" post.

## Why this generalizes

The broader claim is that **agent engineering is unusually prone to Bitter Lesson failure** because the field is iterating in months, not years. A system designed around "the model can't do reliable tool-calling" today is wrong by next quarter. A multi-agent system designed around "the model can't keep coherent context for long-horizon tasks" today is wrong as soon as the next context-management improvement lands. The teams that win are the ones whose architectures can absorb capability gains, not the ones that wrap workarounds around current limitations.

## Practical takeaway

When you're designing an agent system, **explicitly enumerate the model-capability assumptions you're making**, and tag each piece of structure with the assumption it depends on. When models improve, you'll know which scaffolding to remove. Prefer composable low-level primitives over high-level frameworks that bake in assumptions. And periodically ask yourself: "If the model could now do tool-calling reliably / handle long context / coordinate across agents, what structure in my system becomes obsolete?" That's the structure that should be designed for removal. The Bitter Lesson rewards architectures that get out of the model's way.

Geoff Huntley's six-month recap of heavy daily AI-coding use, written from the bleeding edge of the adoption curve. The post is partly a personal story, partly a labor-market warning, and partly a practical primer on what "AI-native engineering" actually looks like in 2025. The thesis is bracing: **"If AI and AI developer tooling were to cease improving today, it would already be good enough to disrupt our profession completely."** The next several years will sort engineers into adopters and resisters, and the gap will compound rapidly.

The "Oh Fuck" moment that names the post: Huntley used Windsurf to convert a Rust audio library to Haskell with GHC 2024, complete with auto-generated FFI bindings, while taking his kids to the pool. He came back to a working library. That experience generalized into a worldview.

## The Fruitco taxonomy

Huntley uses fruit metaphors to describe how organizations are sorting. Skeptics (Pineapple, Grape) dismiss AI; adopters (Orange, Strawberry, Banana) take productivity wins; non-adopters get downgraded as peers' baselines surge. The result is natural attrition between the two camps. He flags Shopify's policy explicitly: "using AI effectively is no longer optional — it's a baseline expectation for employment." Canva restructured technical writing and redesigned engineering interviews to require agent-building skills.

The meta-claim is sharper than the examples: "The baseline expectation of what constitutes high performance is going to shift rapidly." In a few quarters, an engineer who was "exceptional in 2024 without AI" is closer to average. The 2025 frontier is AI-native engineers who automate their own development loop.

## The practices that matter

Huntley names a handful of concrete habits adopters have in common:

**Deliberate, intentional practice.** Not passive tool use — playful, off-the-cuff experiments. Build a COBOL calculator. Make an emoji-based RPN calculator. Write a Comic Sans Chrome extension. "Play and experimentation unlock AI's full potential." This is how you build intuition for what models can and can't do, which is a skill you can't read your way to.

**500-line custom agents.** Roughly 300–400 line personal agents are now table stakes. Canva includes agent-building in technical interviews alongside classic algorithm questions. The point isn't that 500 lines is a magic number — it's that you should be able to write one from scratch when needed, the way you'd write a quicksort.

**Prompt libraries.** Versioned, reusable prompts that enforce consistency across a team. Huntley cites Amp building a Svelte 5 enforcement prompt because Claude defaulted to Svelte 4. LLMs respond predictably to programmatic constraint; team-shared prompts make that constraint applicable to everyone.

**Context window management.** Treat context like C memory: restart fresh rather than extending a problematic conversation. He likens recent subagent improvements to JVM-era garbage-collection improvements — the runtime gets smarter, so the patterns to manage memory get less manual.

## The harder claims

Huntley makes several uncomfortable observations:

- **Identity erasure for specialists.** AI flattens backend/frontend/language distinctions. Decades of specialization translate into less of a moat than they did.
- **Open source disruption.** He generates code directly rather than pulling dependencies; others report the same. The ecosystem dynamics that incentivized small libraries are weakening.
- **IP risk.** AI can function as a "Bitcoin mixer" for IP — companies face real risk if departing engineers use AI loops to replicate functionality at better unit economics.
- **Trust at scale unsolved.** Verifying AI output at the speed it's produced is an open problem. "Vibe coding" — shipping unverified AI output — is reckless. But the verification tooling hasn't caught up to the generation tooling.

On model differentiation, he notes specialization is real: Grok for red-team work (minimal safety restrictions), Anthropic for agentic automation (strong tool-calling), Gemini for document summarization (large context). One vendor for everything is no longer the right default.

## Practical takeaway

Huntley's closing imperative: "This is a perilous year to be complacent, especially at high-performance companies." Concretely: practice deliberately, build your own ~500-line agents, maintain a prompt library, manage context like memory, and ship things. The deeper takeaway is that **AI-native engineering is a skill stack you have to build piece by piece**, not a tool you adopt. The adopters won't out-talk the resisters; they'll just out-ship them, and the gap is already visible in 2025. Read it as a personal practice guide first, and a labor-market forecast second.

Hugging Face's release of **ScreenEnv**, a Dockerized sandboxed Ubuntu desktop for computer-use agents. The thesis is straightforward and practical: building a desktop-control agent requires a controlled environment, and spinning up a VM per session is painful. ScreenEnv compresses that down to a Docker container with **sub-10-second spin-up**, exposes both a direct Python API and an MCP server, and works on AMD64 and ARM64 out of the box.

The value proposition stacks neatly. Full desktop control (mouse, keyboard, window management, file ops, terminal access, screen recording) on demand. Isolated, reproducible environments — agents can run wild without touching your host. Easy deployment anywhere Docker runs. The Dockerization is the key engineering call, because it removes the VM tooling chasm that used to gate this kind of work.

## The one-line API

```python
from screenenv import Sandbox
sandbox = Sandbox()  # That's it!
```

That's the entire setup. The Sandbox object exposes desktop primitives — `left_click(x, y)`, `write(text)`, `press(key)`, `launch(app_name)`, `screenshot()`, `close()`. The directness matters because the developer's mental model is "the desktop is an object I can call methods on," not "I need to wire up VNC and parse pixel grids."

For agent integration, ScreenEnv offers two paths:

**Direct Sandbox API**, where your agent code calls methods on the sandbox directly. Suitable for custom harnesses where you control the agent loop.

**MCP server**, where ScreenEnv exposes the desktop as a Model Context Protocol server and any MCP client (Claude, custom agents, etc.) can connect to it. This is the cleaner integration path for production because it decouples agent and environment.

## Tool implementation pattern

The `DesktopAgentBase` class lets you define agent tools that wrap sandbox actions:

```python
@tool
def click(x: int, y: int) -> str:
    self.desktop.left_click(x, y)
    return f"Clicked at ({x}, {y})"

@tool
def write(text: str) -> str:
    self.desktop.write(text, delay_in_ms=10)
    return f"Typed text: '{text}'"
```

The pattern matches the action vocabulary that GUI-trained models (like Smol2Operator) expect: `click(x, y)`, `write(text)`, `press(key)`, `launch_app(name)`. Action-space alignment between training data and runtime tools is non-trivial; ScreenEnv chooses the same normalized vocabulary, making it a natural fit for the small GUI models people are now training.

## Why this matters in 2025–2026

Computer-use is the agent capability with the most awkward operational story. Most other agent tools (code execution, file I/O, web browsing) have well-established sandbox patterns. Desktop control didn't, because the sandbox had to be a full graphical OS. The pre-ScreenEnv options were heavy: KVM/QEMU VMs, RDP into a managed Windows VM, custom xvfb setups. None of those compose well with Python-based agent loops.

ScreenEnv's contribution is engineering plumbing: a Docker image with Ubuntu + XFCE + accessibility daemons + an MCP server, exposed via a clean Python API, fast to start. None of that is novel research — it's the kind of "glue everything together so the rest of us can move" work that unlocks downstream experimentation. The under-10-second spin-up specifically is what enables CI-style workflows: spin up a sandbox per test, run the agent, tear it down.

## Practical takeaway

If you're building or training a computer-use agent in 2026, **ScreenEnv is your default environment**. Spin up, expose either via Python API or MCP, train and evaluate against it. For training data generation, you can run many parallel containers and collect trajectories cheaply. For deployment, the same image runs in production — no environment drift between dev and prod, which is a real and underrated problem for desktop agents.

The broader point: **computer-use is moving from "specialized lab work" to "normal Docker workflow"**, and that change happens because someone does the unglamorous packaging. ScreenEnv is what that looks like.

E2B's deep technical comparison of **Firecracker vs QEMU** — the two leading microVM technologies for running untrusted code. The post is engineering due-diligence, written by a team that operates one of the larger code-execution sandboxes in the agent ecosystem. The thesis: both leverage KVM, both can run isolated VMs, but the design priorities and resulting tradeoffs are dramatically different — and for ephemeral agent workloads, Firecracker wins decisively.

## The architecture in one paragraph

Both Firecracker and QEMU sit on top of **KVM (Kernel-based Virtual Machine)**, which turns Linux into a Type 1 hypervisor and provides near-native CPU performance. The divergence is at the VMM (Virtual Machine Monitor) layer:

- **Firecracker**: ~**50,000 lines of Rust**, single-process, streamlined API for VM lifecycle management. Built by AWS for Lambda and Fargate.
- **QEMU**: ~**2 million lines of C**, comprehensive hypervisor supporting both pure emulation and KVM acceleration, originating in the early 2000s.

The order-of-magnitude codebase difference is the key fact. Less code means less attack surface, less startup overhead, and easier auditability — but also fewer features.

## Performance numbers

Firecracker's specs are aggressive:

- **Boot time**: "as little as 125ms" cold-start; "3x faster than QEMU MicroVMs and 10x faster than normal QEMU emulation."
- **Memory overhead**: "less than 5MB RAM" per microVM.

QEMU MicroVMs occupy a middle ground — roughly 4x faster than standard QEMU but still 3x slower than Firecracker. For workloads where you spin VMs up and down per request (agent code execution, ephemeral sandboxes for tool calls), the 125ms boot is the load-bearing number. At sub-second boot, you can treat VMs as request-scoped resources; at multi-second boot, you have to pool them and accept the leakage between users.

## The security argument

Firecracker's minimalism is a security feature. Less code means fewer CVEs over time, easier formal review, and a smaller surface for kernel-escape exploits. QEMU's 2M-line codebase has a long CVE history precisely because it tries to support every legacy device and emulation mode under the sun. For untrusted-code execution (running arbitrary agent output, untrusted user submissions), Firecracker's reduced attack surface is the right tradeoff.

Device emulation tradeoffs are where the philosophies diverge most clearly. **Firecracker**: minimal device support, no legacy hardware (no floppy disks, no PCIe passthrough, no GPU). Optimized for modern Linux guests doing modern work. **QEMU**: "Extensive Platform Support" — supports ancient s390x systems, runs old Windows installs, emulates obscure hardware. A different audience entirely.

## When to choose which

The post's recommendation framework is clear:

- **Firecracker**: serverless, edge, ephemeral untrusted-code execution where speed and minimal resource consumption matter. This is the agent-tool-execution sweet spot.
- **QEMU**: when you need versatility, legacy system support, full hardware emulation, or rich device compatibility (development VMs, retro computing, complex test environments).

For the agent ecosystem specifically — running model-generated code, executing notebook cells, hosting computer-use sandboxes — Firecracker is the clear default. The combination of fast boot, low memory overhead, and minimal attack surface is exactly what per-request sandboxes need.

## Practical takeaway

If you're building an agent platform that runs untrusted code, **Firecracker is the right default and the 125ms boot is what makes per-request VMs feasible**. You don't pool, you spawn per task. You don't share VMs across users, you isolate. The economics work because each VM is cheap. QEMU only enters the conversation if you genuinely need device variety (GPUs, complex networking, ancient guest OSes) — for the bulk of agent work, you don't.

The deeper lesson is that **the choice of hypervisor is a load-bearing infrastructure decision for agent platforms**, and it's one most teams adopt before they understand the tradeoffs. If you're picking a sandbox provider — E2B, Modal, Daytona, Replit — knowing whether they're on Firecracker or QEMU under the hood tells you a lot about their cold-start latency, isolation strength, and per-VM cost structure. The agent era is rediscovering microVM engineering as a first-class concern.

Thomas Ptacek's pointed defense of **agentic coding**, written for an audience of skeptical engineers (his "AI Skeptic Friends"). The thesis: the version of LLM coding most skeptics dismiss — pasting half-broken ChatGPT output into your editor — is genuinely bad, but it's also not what serious people are doing in 2025. The agentic version is qualitatively different, and the difference matters enough that dismissing it is a category error.

Ptacek's writing style does a lot of the work. The post is sharp, sometimes confrontational, and unwilling to soft-pedal the implications — both the upside for productive engineers and the genuine displacement risk for the profession. It became the most-cited essay in the 2025 "is AI coding for real" debate precisely because Ptacek refuses to hedge.

## The distinction that matters

The old model: ask ChatGPT for code, paste the result into your editor, fix the broken parts. Ptacek concedes this is genuinely mediocre. Code from a chat window without grounding in your codebase, without execution feedback, without iteration, often invents APIs and produces near-misses.

The new model: **agents "get to poke around your codebase on their own. They author files directly."** They navigate the filesystem with standard Unix tools. They use git. They run compilers, tests, linters. They feed errors back into their own loop. Critically: **"Agents lint. They compile and run tests. If their LLM invents a new function signature, the agent sees the error. They feed it back to the LLM, which says 'oh, right, I totally made that up' and then tries again."**

This is the core empirical claim. Agents are not stochastic text generators producing maybe-correct prose. They are loops grounded in executable reality, where the model proposes and the environment disposes. The same LLM that hallucinates an API in a chat window discovers the hallucination when the compiler rejects it and corrects course. The reliability properties are completely different.

## The strongest arguments

**On project initiation:** Ptacek notes that LLMs eliminate the friction of starting. "An LLM can be instructed to just figure all that shit out. Often, it will drop you precisely at that golden moment where shit almost works." The blank-page tax of starting any non-trivial project — boilerplate, build config, initial scaffolding — is the part LLMs eat for breakfast.

**On code quality:** "Gemini's floor is higher than my own." He's not claiming the LLM is better than him at his peak — he's claiming it has a higher *floor*. Mediocre LLM output beats mediocre human output on most metrics that actually matter (boilerplate completeness, test coverage of obvious cases, consistent formatting). This is the empirical observation that drives a lot of senior-engineer adoption: the LLM doesn't have to be brilliant if it's reliably above-average.

**On the craftsmanship objection:** Ptacek bluntly refuses to engage with the "coding is art" framing. "Professional software developers are in the business of solving practical problems for people with code." Code is a means; the artistic-process argument is a vanity defense.

**On displacement:** Refreshingly, he doesn't pretend the productivity gains are free. "LLMs really might displace many software developers. That's not a high horse we get to ride." The honesty is part of why the essay carries weight — he's not selling a frictionless future, he's describing a regime where some people gain leverage and others get squeezed.

## What's not in the essay

Ptacek deliberately doesn't engage with most of the standard "AI is bad" counter-arguments — quality concerns about specific outputs, legal questions about training data, alignment worries — because his point is upstream of those. The skeptic argument he's targeting is "AI coding is hype, the output is bad, real engineers don't need it." That argument is, in his telling, talking about a category of tool that is no longer the cutting edge.

## Practical takeaway

If you're a senior engineer who tried ChatGPT-coding in 2023 and concluded the technology wasn't there, **the 2025 agent loop is qualitatively different and you should re-evaluate**. Specifically: an agent that can grep your codebase, run your tests, and iterate on compile errors is solving a different problem than a chat window. The honest version of the skeptic position has to engage with that distinction. The honest version of the enthusiast position has to acknowledge the displacement risk. Ptacek's essay is the canonical statement of both, in the same piece. Read it, and stop pretending the 2023 critique still applies to the 2025 technology.

LangChain's argument for a third layer in the agent stack: **interpreters that sit between raw tool calls and full sandboxes**. The thesis is that serial tool-call orchestration is wasteful and full sandboxes give the agent too much capability for most needs. A scoped JavaScript interpreter — they use QuickJS — splits the difference: agents get code-level composition over a narrow, allowlisted set of tools, without inheriting an entire OS environment.

The framing: "Interpreters sit between serial tool calls and full sandboxes. Agents get code-level composition over scoped capabilities without inheriting a whole environment." That's the design space succinctly named. Below this layer: traditional function-calling, one tool at a time, with message-passing between turns. Above: full sandboxes (E2B, ScreenEnv, Firecracker microVMs) that give the agent shell access, network, filesystem, package install. In between: an interpreter with explicit tool bridges and no implicit ambient capabilities.

## The design principles

**Intentional constraints.** The interpreter deliberately *lacks* filesystem access, network access, shell, and package installation by default. This is the inverse of the sandbox approach (start broad, restrict by policy). Here you start narrow and the developer must explicitly expose capabilities. The result is a much smaller and more reasoned-about attack surface.

**Three context surfaces.** LangChain distinguishes between:
- **Message history** — the model's reasoning trace, conversation turns, tool call records.
- **Filesystem** — durable artifacts that persist across turns.
- **Interpreter state** — live working values in the JS runtime, not in the model context.

The split matters because **intermediate state in the interpreter doesn't clutter the model's context window**. If the agent processes a 10,000-row dataset, the rows live in interpreter memory; only the summary or result gets surfaced to the model. This is a quietly important architecture move — most agent loops force every intermediate value into the model context, which is exactly backwards.

## Programmatic Tool Calling (PTC)

The concrete mechanism is **Programmatic Tool Calling**: allowlisted tools are exposed inside the interpreter as async functions in a `tools` namespace. The agent writes JavaScript that calls those tools, processes results in code, and composes them. The headline number from early testing: **"up to 35% fewer tokens on some tasks"** versus traditional sequential tool calling.

The token savings come from eliminating the round-trips where the model sees each intermediate tool result and decides what to do next. With PTC, the model writes a small program ("call tool A, filter the results by X, call tool B with the filtered set, return the count"), the interpreter runs it, the model sees only the final result. For high-fan-out workloads (process 50 items, each requiring a tool call), the difference is dramatic.

## Why QuickJS specifically

QuickJS is the right choice because it's:
- Embeddable into any host process
- Pure JavaScript runtime, no Node.js APIs by default
- Supports persistent runtime context across multiple `eval` calls (REPL-like behavior)
- Configurable memory limits and execution timeouts (safe defaults)
- Snapshotting for fast resume

The REPL-like persistence matters because the agent can run code, see the result, then run more code that references the previous result — all without round-tripping through the model. The interpreter becomes a true working surface, not just a one-shot evaluator.

## Practical takeaway

If your agent does work that involves **composing multiple tool calls, filtering/processing intermediate results, or iterating over collections**, consider adding an interpreter layer rather than handling each step as a model turn. The token savings are real (35% on representative tasks), the architectural cleanliness is significant (intermediate state out of model context), and you avoid the security overhead of a full sandbox.

The deeper point is that **agent runtimes are starting to specialize into layered architectures** — interpreter for composition, sandbox for arbitrary code, MCP for typed access to live systems, Skills for packaged knowledge. The choice between them isn't which one is "best"; it's matching the capability and trust profile to the task. LangChain's contribution here is naming and implementing the interpreter layer cleanly, and the PTC pattern is likely to spread to other agent runtimes in 2026.

The launch post for the **OpenHands Software Agent SDK** — an open-source Python framework for building reproducible coding agents. The thesis is that production coding agents need a stable, pluggable substrate: configurable LLM backends, composable tools, custom prompts, persistent conversations, and isolation primitives. OpenHands is positioning itself as the open alternative to closed coding-agent products (Devin, Codex, Claude Code) for teams that want full control over the loop.

## The core abstractions

**LLM**: Models are configured through a unified interface. The canonical example: `LLM(model="anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929", api_key=...)`. Provider-neutral, so the same agent code works with Anthropic, OpenAI, open-weights models served via Ollama, etc. This decoupling matters because **model choice should be a config decision, not an architecture decision** — too many agent frameworks bake in a single provider.

**Tools**: Composable building blocks following an action/observation pattern. Built-in options cover terminal execution, file editing, and task tracking. Developers can extend with custom tools by implementing matching action and observation classes. The pattern matches the broader industry direction (clean separation between what the agent decides to do and what the environment reports back).

**Agent**: The composition layer. Takes an LLM instance and an array of tools, with no rigid orchestration imposed. You assemble the agent you want from primitives rather than configuring a pre-built one.

**Conversation**: Maintains agent state, workspace context, and the message flow between user requests and agent responses. Conversations are first-class objects — you can persist them, restore them, and continue them across sessions via a persistence directory and conversation IDs.

## The architectural choices that matter

**Custom extensions follow a standard pattern.** Rather than requiring a plugin SDK, OpenHands lets developers "extend agent capabilities with your own tools using standard action/observation patterns." This keeps the extension surface narrow and predictable — define an action class, define the matching observation class, register the tool. No special framework knowledge required.

**Skill injection for contextual knowledge.** Domain-specific guidance can be triggered conditionally — by keyword match, by tool use, by user intent. This is the same Skills pattern Claude popularized, applied to an open framework. The model only loads the skill content when it's relevant, keeping the default token cost low.

**Persistence is first-class.** Conversations can be saved and restored using a persistence directory and conversation IDs. This is what enables long-running agent workflows — a session can pause, the user can come back hours later, and the agent resumes with full state. Anthropic's long-running harness post advocates the same principle; OpenHands bakes it into the SDK.

**Docker isolation.** Workspaces can run in "isolated Docker containers for enhanced security and scalability." The pattern matches what ScreenEnv does for desktop agents and what Anthropic's sandboxing does for Claude Code: agent runtime is something you isolate, not something you trust.

## Where OpenHands fits in the ecosystem

OpenHands has been a notable open-source coding-agent project (formerly OpenDevin), and the SDK is the natural productization. The positioning is clear: if you want a Claude-Code-like experience but with full source access, your own model choice, and the ability to customize the loop, this is the path. The pluggable LLM is critical for teams that need to run open-weights or self-hosted models for cost, privacy, or sovereignty reasons.

The tradeoff against closed products: more setup, more decisions, no managed sandbox. The win: no vendor lock-in, no telemetry leaking your code, full control over what tools and prompts the agent uses.

## Practical takeaway

If you're building a coding agent and you want full control without writing the harness from scratch, **OpenHands SDK is one of the few credible open options** alongside the lower-level LangChain/LangGraph stack. Start with the default tool set, swap in your preferred LLM provider, customize the prompts to your codebase conventions, and add Docker isolation when you're running anything untrusted. The deeper architectural lesson is that **pluggable LLM, composable tools, action/observation patterns, persistent conversations, and Docker isolation are now the consensus primitives for coding-agent frameworks** — every serious player converges on roughly the same shape, and OpenHands is the open implementation of that shape.

Glean's writeup of their **agentic reasoning architecture** for the enterprise. The thesis is that effective enterprise agents are built on three layered components: a strong search/retrieval foundation, a prompt-builder layer that encodes business processes, and an LLM-agnostic reasoning layer that adapts those processes to novel situations. The post is positioning, but the architectural argument is substantive enough to be useful even if you're not a Glean customer.

## Search as the foundation

The load-bearing claim is that **enterprise agents are search problems first**. Glean's foundation is a knowledge layer that "indexes the entirety of company knowledge, spread across potentially hundreds of SaaS applications, data repositories, and communications systems." Slack, Jira, Confluence, Google Drive, Salesforce, internal wikis, codebases. Without comprehensive retrieval, the agent operates from intuition over a fraction of the relevant context, which in an enterprise setting means hallucination by default.

The practical implication is that companies trying to build agents on top of just an LLM, without an enterprise-grade retrieval substrate, are solving the wrong problem first. The agent quality ceiling is set by the search quality. Glean's pitch is that they spent years building the cross-source index, and any reasoning layer built on top inherits that scope.

## Prompt builder for encoded business processes

The middle layer is the **prompt builder** — a visual tool that combines building blocks (retrieve from the index, perform an LLM reasoning step, call a SaaS API) into reusable workflows. "The system visually creates these prompts by combining building blocks that retrieve content from the Glean index with LLM reasoning steps," transforming organizational knowledge into repeatable artifacts.

The argument is that **most enterprise work is encoded process, not novel reasoning**: onboarding flows, ticket triage, expense classification, RFP generation, vendor evaluation. These should be captured once by a domain expert into a structured workflow, then executed reliably by agents. The prompt builder is essentially an authoring tool for SOPs that LLMs can execute, which is the same pattern the OpenAI cookbook "routine generation" post advocates — except authored by humans through a UI rather than compiled from KB articles by o1.

## LLM-agnostic agentic reasoning on top

The top layer is true agentic reasoning, which Glean explicitly designs to be **LLM-agnostic**. Multiple models for different tasks, swappable as the frontier moves. The reasoning layer handles cases where the encoded workflows don't fit — "adapt known patterns to novel situations while maintaining alignment with organizational practices and preferences."

Glean's design balances **generalization vs. specialization** across the action space. Specialized agents handle narrow, well-understood domain tasks efficiently; general reasoning addresses novel scenarios. "Successful enterprise AI agent architectures will likely need to support both paradigms." This is correct as a framing — pure-generalist agents are inefficient on common tasks, pure-specialist setups can't handle the long tail. The architecture has to span both.

## What's notable beyond the marketing

The architectural claim that **enterprise agents are layered systems with search at the base, encoded workflows in the middle, and free-form reasoning on top** is genuinely useful as a reference architecture. Many enterprise AI projects fail because they collapse this into one layer — they try to do everything via prompt engineering on top of generic retrieval, with no encoded business logic. The result is agents that hallucinate process steps that don't exist at the company.

The LLM-agnostic positioning is also worth noting. In 2026, enterprise buyers don't want vendor lock-in to a single model provider. Architectures that present "the LLM is replaceable" as a first-class property will win procurement debates that ones tied to a specific model won't.

## Practical takeaway

If you're architecting an enterprise agent platform, **invest in retrieval first, encoded workflows second, and free-form reasoning third — in that order**. Many teams reverse the order, leading with model choice and prompt engineering, and discovering too late that the agent has nothing useful to retrieve from. Adopt the layered model: cross-system index, prompt-builder for repeatable processes (so domain experts can author SOPs that aren't prose), and an agent loop that can fall back to general reasoning when the workflow library doesn't cover the case. The deeper point is that **enterprise AI is mostly an information-routing and process-encoding problem**, and the model is the cheapest component of the stack — exactly the opposite of how most startups pitch their value.

## Thesis

For open-ended research tasks where the path can't be pre-planned, a **lead-orchestrator plus parallel subagents** outperforms a single agent by a wide margin — but only if you accept that the system burns roughly 15x the tokens of a chat turn and demands an entirely new operational stack.

## What the post actually shows

Anthropic's headline number: multi-agent Claude Opus 4 (lead) with Sonnet 4 subagents beat single-agent Opus 4 by **90.2%** on their internal research evals. The mechanism is mundane — parallel context windows mean three to five subagents can explore breadth-first (e.g., "find every board member across S&P 500 IT companies") while the lead synthesizes. They report that **token usage alone explains ~80% of performance variance** in browsing tasks; multi-agent uses roughly 15x the tokens of a chat message versus 4x for single-agent research. That's the explicit cost-benefit trade: pay 4x more tokens than a solo agent to unlock the breadth-first parallelism.

The prompting lessons are unusually concrete. **Delegation must be detailed**: "research X" causes duplication and gaps, so subagents get explicit objectives, output formats, tool guidance, and stop conditions. Effort must be **explicitly scaled** — simple fact-finding gets 1 agent with 3-10 tool calls, complex research gets 10+ subagents with divided territories. They claim Claude itself can diagnose its own prompt failures and propose fixes, **cutting task completion time by 40%**. Parallel tool calls plus 3-5 parallel subagents shaved research time **up to 90%**. Extended thinking acts as a scratchpad for subagents to evaluate result quality between tool calls.

The production section is the most useful part of the post. Statefulness compounds errors — you can't just restart a 30-minute trace, so they checkpoint and let agents adapt to tool failures rather than crash. Debugging is hard because runs are non-reproducible: they invested in full production tracing and **decision-pattern monitoring without recording conversation content**. Deploys use rainbow patterns to avoid disrupting in-flight agents. Subagent execution is currently synchronous (the lead blocks on the slowest worker), which they flag as future work.

## Evaluating non-deterministic traces

Classic step-validation fails because legitimate paths diverge. Their answer: start with **20 representative queries**, which is enough to reveal 30%-to-80% jumps; use **LLM-as-judge with a single rubric prompt** scoring accuracy, citations, completeness, source quality, and tool efficiency on a 0.0-1.0 scale. Human testing remained essential — it caught a bias toward SEO-optimized content farms over authoritative sources that the LLM judges missed entirely.

## Memorable quotes

> "Agent-tool interfaces are as critical as human-computer interfaces."

> "The gap between prototype and production is often wider than anticipated."

## Practical takeaway

Don't reach for multi-agent until your task is genuinely breadth-first and you can defend the 15x token bill. When you do, invest in (a) extremely detailed delegation prompts that scope each subagent's territory, (b) durable execution with checkpointing and rainbow deploys, and (c) a tiny LLM-judge eval over ~20 queries before chasing larger datasets. The honest read is that multi-agent is a niche pattern with a large cost multiplier, not a default architecture.

## Thesis

Skills are a new primitive for shipping procedural knowledge to agents: a directory containing a `SKILL.md` plus optional reference docs and scripts, **progressively disclosed** so the model only loads what a task actually needs. They're complementary to MCP, not a replacement — MCP connects external systems, Skills package internal expertise.

## How progressive disclosure works

Anthropic lays out three layers that load on demand:

1. **Metadata layer** — the skill's name and one-line description sit in the system prompt at startup. This is the discovery signal that tells Claude *when* to engage the skill.
2. **Core documentation** — the full `SKILL.md` activates only after the metadata triggers and Claude decides the skill is relevant.
3. **Referenced assets** — supplementary files (`forms.md`, `reference.md`, helper scripts) load lazily when the workflow inside `SKILL.md` calls for them.

The analogy in the post is **a well-organized manual with a table of contents, chapters, and a detailed appendix** — you don't read the appendix until you need it. The PDF skill is their canonical example: a small `SKILL.md` references separate form-filling and general reference docs, and Claude only pulls the form section when it's actually filling a form.

## Skills vs. MCP

The distinction is sharp: **Skills teach agents complex workflows; MCP connects them to external systems**. A skill might encode "how this company does code review" or "the seven-step legal contract review process," packaged as markdown plus optional bash scripts. MCP provides tool surfaces against Jira, GitHub, or a database. Skills can *use* MCP tools — they're orthogonal abstractions on different axes.

## Why this matters as a primitive

The deeper point is about context economics. Bundling everything an agent might need into a system prompt wrecks the KV-cache and dilutes attention. Bundling nothing forces the agent to discover capabilities from scratch every turn. Progressive disclosure splits the difference: enough metadata to know what exists, full content only when used. It's the file-system-as-memory pattern (popularized by Manus) extended into a packaging format that organizations can version and share.

## Memorable quotes

> "Skills extend Claude's capabilities by packaging your expertise into composable resources... transforming general-purpose agents into specialized agents that fit your needs."

## Practical takeaway

If you're already maintaining sprawling system prompts or shipping the same procedural instructions across multiple tools, restructure them as Skills. Start with the metadata: a tight one-line description that names the trigger condition. Push deterministic logic into scripts the skill calls (formatters, validators, generators) and keep `SKILL.md` focused on interpretation and tool composition. The pattern works inside the file system today and is the obvious place organizations should put their company-specific agent know-how.

## Thesis

Three new tool-use features — **Tool Search Tool**, **Programmatic Tool Calling**, and **Tool Use Examples** — attack three distinct context bloat problems. Together they cut token usage by **up to 85%** on complex agents while *improving* accuracy on Anthropic's internal benchmarks.

## The three features and what each fixes

**Tool Search Tool** addresses upfront tool-definition bloat. Instead of stuffing every tool schema into the system prompt, the model calls a search tool to discover relevant tools on demand. Anthropic claims it preserves **95% of the context window** by loading only what's needed. Internal results: Opus 4 jumped from 49% to 74% accuracy; Opus 4.5 from 79.5% to 88.1%. The headline 85% token reduction is the marketing line, but the accuracy improvement is the more interesting data point — fewer tools in context apparently means *better* selection.

**Programmatic Tool Calling** is the more architecturally novel feature. Rather than returning every tool result to the model's context, Claude writes Python code in a sandboxed executor that orchestrates the calls and only returns synthesized results. Their numbers: a **37% token reduction** on complex research (43,588 → 27,297 tokens) with accuracy on knowledge tasks climbing from 25.6% to 28.5%. This is the same pattern as the Recursive Language Models work — keep heavy intermediate results outside the context window, expose them as Python variables.

**Tool Use Examples** addresses what JSON Schema can't express: how parameters actually correlate, format conventions, edge cases. Concrete few-shot tool invocations bumped accuracy on complex parameter handling **from 72% to 90%**. This is just structured few-shotting for tool calls, but it's evidence the model needs more than typed schemas to use APIs correctly.

## Why it matters

Each feature targets a different bottleneck and they compose. Tool Search shrinks the *definition* footprint. Programmatic Tool Calling shrinks the *result* footprint. Tool Use Examples improves *call* accuracy without growing either. Anthropic frames these as additive — the 85% claim implies stacking the savings on the right workload.

The contrarian read: this is Anthropic acknowledging that bigger context windows aren't the answer for agents. The right move is to *avoid putting stuff in context in the first place* — either by lazy-loading definitions, executing logic in a sandbox, or teaching the model patterns up front instead of having it figure them out from schemas alone.

## Practical takeaway

If you're shipping an agent with more than ~20 tools, Tool Search Tool is now table stakes — the accuracy numbers alone justify it. If your agent does heavy multi-call workflows (data analysis, multi-step API orchestration), Programmatic Tool Calling lets you turn N round trips into one with code that pages through results without paying token costs. Tool Use Examples should be the default for any tool whose schema doesn't fully express the semantics. The three features represent the strongest signal yet that the future of "tool use" is closer to letting the model write and execute code against your tools than to expanding the JSON schema surface.

## Thesis

MCP replaces the **M×N integration explosion** between AI applications and data sources with a single open protocol. Built on JSON-RPC, with server/client SDKs and reference implementations, it's the foundation Anthropic put in place for everything the agent ecosystem has built on top since November 2024.

## What MCP solves

The announcement frames the problem starkly: "every new data source requires its own custom implementation, making truly connected systems difficult to scale." Every assistant needed bespoke connectors to every data source — N applications times M sources of custom glue code. MCP collapses that to N + M: applications connect once to MCP, data sources expose themselves once via MCP servers, and the bipartite graph dissolves.

The architecture is straightforward: **two roles, one wire format**. Developers either build an **MCP server** exposing data, tools, or prompts, or build an **MCP client** (an AI application) that connects to those servers. The protocol itself is JSON-RPC, intentionally boring and battle-tested. The choice signals Anthropic wanted broad adoption, not lock-in.

## What launched on day one

The initial release shipped:

- **Specification and SDKs** on GitHub
- **Local MCP server support in Claude Desktop**, the first reference client
- **Pre-built servers** for Google Drive, Slack, GitHub, Postgres, and Puppeteer — covering the canonical "plug an LLM into the things developers already use" surface

Early partners included **Block and Apollo** integrating into internal systems, plus developer-tool companies — **Zed, Replit, Codeium, and Sourcegraph** — committing to MCP support in coding assistants.

## Memorable quotes

> "The result is a simpler, more reliable way to give AI systems access to the data they need."

## Why this post matters retrospectively

In the year that followed, MCP became the de facto agent integration standard. Every "how we built our agent" post — Ramp, Cloudflare, Continue, Block, Stripe — references MCP servers somewhere in the stack. OpenAI's Agents SDK supports MCP. The open-source server registry on GitHub has hundreds of community-contributed connectors. This post is the genesis document for that ecosystem, even though at launch it read as a modest protocol announcement with a Claude Desktop demo.

The contrarian read: MCP succeeded because Anthropic made it boring on purpose. JSON-RPC over stdio or HTTP, no custom auth model, no opinionated DSL. The protocol does the minimum a protocol must do, which is exactly what made it adoptable.

## Practical takeaway

If you're shipping an agent today and you've written custom integrations against three or more external services, you should be evaluating whether to refactor them as MCP servers. The benefit isn't internal — it's that the same servers become reusable across every MCP-capable client (Claude Desktop, Cursor, Zed, Codex, your own agent). If you're exposing data your customers' agents need to read, ship an MCP server. The investment is small, the surface area is well-defined, and the ecosystem has converged enough that it's a one-way door worth walking through.

## Thesis

Claude 3.7 introduces a single model that can think for as long as the developer asks it to, with a **developer-controllable thinking budget** and a **visible scratchpad**. Parallel test-time compute — sampling many thought chains and voting — pushes performance further but at heavy cost.

## How the thinking budget works

The feature isn't a separate "reasoning model" routed to behind the scenes — it's the same Claude 3.7 with a toggle. Users see a raw, unfiltered thought process; developers set the maximum thinking tokens. Anthropic reports that **accuracy on math problems improves logarithmically** with allocated thinking tokens, but with an interesting caveat: Claude "generally stops short" of using the full budget when it doesn't need it. The model self-throttles, which is the desired behavior — pay for thinking only when thinking helps.

The philosophical choice here is worth noting: the visible thought process is **raw and unfiltered**, deliberately bypassing character training. The reasoning: forcing the model to be "in character" while thinking degrades the thinking. The exception is a small set of safety-sensitive categories (CSAM, cyber attacks, weapons), where the thought process is encrypted with a placeholder.

## Parallel test-time compute results

The research portion of the post is where the real numbers live. Sampling multiple independent thought chains and either majority-voting or selecting via a learned scoring model substantially boosts accuracy. The published result: **84.8% on GPQA with 256 samples at 64k thinking tokens each**, hitting **96.5% on the physics subset**. This is brute-force test-time compute — the equivalent of running the model hundreds of times and picking the consensus answer. Practical for high-stakes one-shot decisions; uneconomic for routine work.

## Agent-relevant signals

Two specific agent benchmarks appear:

- **OSWorld** (computer use): Claude 3.7 "improves faster over interaction steps than Claude 3.5" — i.e., extended thinking pays off more as the trajectory grows.
- **Pokémon Red** as an agentic stress test: 3.7 defeated three gym leaders, where previous versions "failed to leave the starting house." Anecdotal but telling — the same model with thinking enabled is qualitatively different on multi-step environments.
- **Prompt injection defense**: 88% attack prevention, up from 74% — a notable side effect of letting the model reason through manipulation attempts.

## Memorable quotes

> "the rest of the thought process is not available for this response."

(The placeholder used when safety filters fire.)

## Practical takeaway

For agents, extended thinking is most valuable as a **scratchpad between tool calls** — letting the model reason about whether the last result was actually useful before deciding the next action. Set a generous-but-bounded thinking budget and trust the model to self-throttle. Skip parallel test-time compute (256 samples at 64k tokens each is roughly the cost of a small fine-tune) unless you're in evaluation mode or solving a one-shot high-stakes problem. The visible-thinking design is also a debugging gift: in production traces you can see *why* the agent made a choice, not just *what* it chose.

## Thesis

Use **agents-as-tools** — not handoffs — when one agent needs to consult specialists. A central Portfolio Manager invokes Macro/Fundamental/Quant analysts as if they were function calls, keeps the single control thread, and mixes function tools, hosted tools, and MCP servers under one roof.

## The agents-as-tools pattern

OpenAI's cookbook draws a sharp distinction between two multi-agent patterns. In **handoffs**, control transfers to another agent — the conversation belongs to whoever was handed the baton. In **agents-as-tools**, "one agent (often a central planner or manager) calls other agents as if they were tools. Sub-agents don't take over the conversation; instead, the main agent invokes them for specific subtasks." The Portfolio Manager remains orchestrator throughout, which gives you (a) parallel execution because specialists are just tool calls, (b) auditable reasoning because every delegation is logged, and (c) a single coherent output.

## The Portfolio Manager architecture

Three hub-and-spoke specialists handle different cognitive territory:

- **Fundamental Analysis** — company financials, business moat, qualitative research
- **Macro Environment** — economic conditions, Fed policy, sector rotation
- **Quantitative Analysis** — technical indicators, correlation, scenario modeling

The Head PM wraps each specialist as a `function_tool` and uses a `run_all_specialists_parallel` helper with `parallel_tool_calls=True` to fan them out concurrently. The results come back, the PM synthesizes them into a unified investment memo. This is roughly the same pattern Anthropic uses in the multi-agent research system, but the cookbook version makes the SDK-level mechanics legible.

## The tool-type triple

The demo deliberately mixes three categories to show they interoperate:

1. **Custom function tools** — Python wrappers around FRED API access, file operations.
2. **Hosted/managed tools** — OpenAI's Code Interpreter for quantitative analysis, WebSearch for live news.
3. **MCP servers** — Yahoo Finance via the Model Context Protocol for external data.

The practical point: an agent system doesn't have to choose one tool format. The Agents SDK treats all three as first-class, which means you can pick the right vehicle per integration (hosted for capability you'd never re-implement, MCP for ecosystem reuse, custom functions for in-house data).

## Design choices worth stealing

- **System prompt as policy** — the PM's instructions live in a `pm_base.md` file encoding firm philosophy, workflow steps, and tool-use rules. Versioning the policy independently of the code is the right call.
- **Auditability** — every tool call is captured in OpenAI Traces, which the post leans on heavily for debugging.
- **Modularity** — each specialist can be updated without touching the others.

## Memorable quotes

> "One agent (often a central planner or manager) calls other agents as if they were tools."

## Practical takeaway

If you've been wiring up handoff-based multi-agent flows and finding them hard to reason about, flip to agents-as-tools.
